title: A Strange Sulfur Planet Found by a Space Telescope
summary: Scientists using the James Webb Space Telescope have found a weird planet far away from Earth. This planet, called L 98-59 d, orbits a red star about 35 light-years away. It is bigger than Earth but has low density and an atmosphere with hydrogen sulfide, which smells like rotten eggs. Unlike other planets, it doesn't fit into known categories. Researchers think it has a deep ocean of molten rock inside, called a magma ocean, that traps lots of sulfur. This magma ocean helps keep a thick atmosphere with sulfur gases. The planet is not like Earth and probably cannot support life, but it shows there are many different kinds of planets in space. This discovery helps scientists learn more about planets beyond our Solar System.

keywords.explanation: a large object in space that orbits a star
a yellow element that can smell bad and is found in gases
a tool that helps scientists see faraway things in space
the layer of gases around a planet
a deep sea of hot, melted rock inside a planet

How far is the planet L 98-59 d from Earth?
What gas in the planet's atmosphere smells like rotten eggs?
What is inside the planet that traps sulfur?
Can the planet L 98-59 d support life?
What does this discovery show about planets in space?

background_read: Planets are big objects that go around stars. Our Solar System has planets like Earth and Mars. Scientists use telescopes to find planets far away, called exoplanets. Some planets have gases around them called atmospheres. Sulfur is a substance that can be in gases and smells bad. A magma ocean is hot, melted rock inside a planet. This article talks about a new planet that is different from others because it has lots of sulfur and a magma ocean.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from the University of Oxford and other universities
WHAT: Found a strange planet with sulfur and a magma ocean
WHERE: 35 light-years from Earth, orbiting a red star
WHY: To learn about different types of planets in space

perspectives.description: This shows how new tools like the James Webb Space Telescope help find unknown planets.
